title: Pulse — SaaS Trial Signup Landing Page
description: An original free-trial signup landing page sample for a fictional product-analytics SaaS — built to test a single-CTA, no-card-required conversion pattern.
longDescription: Pulse is a self-initiated landing-page sample, not a real product. I built it to apply the same conversion-focused layout discipline I use for client PPC pages: one clear CTA above the fold, friction-reducing copy ('no credit card required'), and a feature grid that answers objections instead of just listing capabilities.
highlights: Single repeated CTA (hero + footer) instead of a multi-link nav, to avoid splitting attention
"No credit card required" placed directly under the CTA to pre-empt the #1 signup objection
Feature grid framed around outcomes (what you see/avoid) rather than feature names alone

caseStudy.problem: SaaS trial pages often lose visitors to decision fatigue — too many nav links, vague feature lists, and an unstated cost of entry (credit card) that creates hesitation before the visitor even reads the value proposition.
caseStudy.approach: Stripped the page down to one path: headline, single email field, one CTA, repeated once more at the bottom. No navbar, no competing links — every element on the page exists to either build trust in the next ten seconds or move the visitor toward the form.
decisions: Removed all navigation — a PPC visitor arrived for one reason, so the page should only offer one next action
Put 'No credit card required · 14-day trial · Cancel anytime' directly beneath the CTA, not buried in fine print, since it answers the most common hesitation at the exact decision point
Used three feature cards instead of a long list — each framed as a benefit statement, not a technical feature name, since PPC traffic skims rather than reads
caseStudy.outcome: // TODO: No real traffic — this is a self-initiated sample, not a deployed campaign. Conversion data not available.
